words: SAM SCHRAGER: The first thing I wanted to ask ya, is do you know why your folks
decided to come out here from Nebraska?
DICK BENGE: Yes sir, I do. You know, dad had poor health, and the doctor told
him that he

speaker: DB
words: etter come to a higher climate. We lived at that time in Nebraska. And that's how

speaker: DB
words: he come to come out here. We all moved out on

speaker: DB
words: dad's account of his health, see. Well, he lived several years after we

speaker: DB
words: came out here. After he come out here and got in this higher climate, why...

speaker: SAM
words: Why'd they pick Idaho?

speaker: DB
words: j Well, now that's another question. Because there's other families that

speaker: DB
words: come from right in there that we knew, that lived here at Princeton. McFarlands

speaker: DB
words: was one of 'era, Bains, McVeys was another one. We knew them there.

speaker: DB
words: So it was just like moving into, they all knew one another. Been raised

speaker: DB
words: together in Missouri was the idea. So that's how that come about (chuckles).

speaker: SAM
words: What do you remember from when you were a kid there, what it was like farming

speaker: SAM
words: in Nebraska and in Missouri?

speaker: DB
words: I don't remember farming any in Missouri, but in Nebraska it was, well,

speaker: DB
words: dry. And the grasshoppers was pretty hard, and when the grasshoppers didn't

speaker: DB
words: get ya sometimes the hailstorms would (chuckles). That's just the way it

speaker: DB
words: was. Boy I'll tell a man, that was a hard country to farm in. Sometime

speaker: DB
words: you raised good crops, sometime you didn't raise much. No.

speaker: SAM
words: How do you'think it'd compare with out here?

speaker: DB
words: Oh this is altogether a different country. Altogether different from what

speaker: DB
words: it was there. You know, we had wind, lightining storms, hailstorms, and

speaker: DB
words: everything (chuckles). Boy I'll tell ya—you know we come out here, and we

speaker: DB
words: hadn't been here very long, and Eldon Tribble (that was Hershiel's brother),

speaker: DB
words: they lived right close to us there. Come up a little windstorm, blowed down

speaker: DB
words: a tree or two, that was before it was logged out. They come up after the

speaker: DB
words: windstorm was over, talking about how the win

speaker: DB
words: lowed. Well, we didn't notice it a-blowin', 'cause the win

speaker: DB
words: lowed that hard every day there (chuckles). And we had a clap or two of thunder,

speaker: DB
words: you know, and well, we

speaker: DB
words: didn't pay much attention to that. They come up and they's talkin' about it,

speaker: DB
words: and we told 'em, "Well that was nothing. We didn't notice it." We was just

speaker: DB
words: joshing, you know. So we told Shorty and Hershiel (Dick's brother-in-law)

speaker: DB
words: both, they was talking, why there was four or five pretty sharp lightning

speaker: DB
words: flashes and claps of thunder, and we told *em, we said that "You oughta go

speaker: DB
words: back to Nebraska and Iowa and Missouri and through that country, if you want

speaker: DB
words: to see electric storm." And I told Shorty, I said "Eldon," I said, "I've

speaker: DB
words: seen it lightning there you almost read rewspaper by it." "Noooo!," he says.

speaker: DB
words: "Well," I said, "all right." So he went back to Iowa, him and his cousin

speaker: DB
words: on a visit, they had some relatives in Iowa. He come back and he come up and

speaker: DB
words: he said, "Dick, I apologize." "What are ya apologizing fer?"

speaker: DB
words: He said, "I thought you was lying about lightning, but," he said, "we

speaker: DB
words: had one while we was there," and he said, "I figured you didn't give it quite

speaker: DB
words: strong enough!" (Laughs.) I'll tell a man they did have some storms there,

speaker: DB
words: lightning and thunder, y'know. I've seen it almost just one flash right after

speaker: DB
words: another one, of thunder y'know, it-d just upset the house there, nearly seemed

speaker: DB
words: like. But we's always used to it. We never paid no attention (chuckles). Yah.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you remember anything about the trip out here?

speaker: DB
words: Dh yeah. Yeah, I come out here, I was on crutches when I come.

speaker: SAM
words: You was on crutches?

speaker: DB
words: Yeah, a horse fell on me an

speaker: DB
words: roke my leg in four places. I was on crutches when we come out here. We left Waymore,

speaker: DB
words: Nebraska, and we come to

speaker: DB
words: Lincoln, and then we got on the Great Northern. Well, we come from Waymore

speaker: DB
words: up to Beatrice on the Burlington, and then they put us on the Great Northern.

speaker: DB
words: Well there was three coaches of us. They just took the same coaches, and

speaker: DB
words: the orderly come through and he said, "You're all gonna have to move back

speaker: DB
words: in another car." And we asked him why. Well he said, "You're goin' right

speaker: DB
words: through," he said, "this three coaches are going through to Seattle." And

speaker: DB
words: he said, "I'll be on it all the time." And he said, "You move back there,

speaker: DB
words: and" he said, "you won't be bothered any more, changing cars nor nothing,

speaker: DB
words: because" he said, "we're gonna keep right on the same train all the time.

speaker: DB
words: Dust take the three coaches and put »em over on the other line and go on."

speaker: DB
words: Well, that was pretty nice. So we come right through to Spokane from Lincoln,

speaker: DB
words: Nebraska right in the same coach.

speaker: SAM
words: So what happened after you got off?

speaker: DB
words: Well (chuckles), we got off in Spokane and then, we's only there oh, seemed

speaker: DB
words: to me like as near as I can remember about an hour, and then we come to

speaker: DB
words: Palouse and stayed all night. Come to Palouse down here and stayed all

speaker: DB
words: night, then we come up the next day at noon on the WIM, that's the one that

speaker: DB
words: runs up through here. Y'know, we'

speaker: DB
words: een used to them potatoes there in Nebraska, they was all dried potatoes y'know,

speaker: DB
words: thought they'd sugared the potatoes the first night after supper. Yeah,

speaker: DB
words: they tasted sweet and nice, y'know. We all talked about it, what'd they

speaker: DB
words: put the sugar in the potatoes fer? Come to find out they's all sugared.

speaker: DB
words: Yeah. 'Cause them potatoes, oh boy, they were strong y'know, dry weather,

speaker: DB
words: not irrigated nor nothing. They didn't know what irrigating was there.

speaker: DB
words: Boy I'll tell a man they was strong. Green, y'know, you know what a green

speaker: DB
words: potato is? You know, when he's green, he's strong. Yeah. So, we had a

speaker: DB
words: nice trip.

speaker: SAM
words: So how did ya come about to locate here, in this place?

speaker: DB
words: Well, I'll tell ya how. We come into Princeton, and Eldon Tribble

speaker: DB
words: (Hershiel's brother), his wife had just passed away. And he had a place down

speaker: DB
words: below and he had to sell it, so da

speaker: DB
words: ought it off of him, right across the

speaker: DB
words: road from where Hershiel lives now. Well he bought the place off of him,

speaker: DB
words: and he stayed right there with mother 'til they both passed away, right

speaker: DB
words: there on the place they bought off of Tribble. Well, my boy owns it now.

speaker: DB
words: Yeah, he owns 80 acres there, and that's the way it all come about.

speaker: SAM
words: Was there a place there when you got there, a building?

speaker: DB
words: Oh yeah, there was a little log house, and one thing another. Good

speaker: DB
words: spring, which helped, and an old log barn.

speaker: SAM
words: And you were getting around on crutches.

speaker: DB
words: Yeah, I was getting around on 'em, I was on crutches there for...Well, I

speaker: DB
words: got hurt in Danuary, and I never got off my crutches 'til April. Because

speaker: DB
words: he told me, he said, "That leg is broke in four places, you've gotta be

speaker: DB
words: careful with it, you got to let all them bones set before ya start." So he

speaker: DB
words: told me what to do, and so after about so long, well I took my knife and

speaker: DB
words: took the cast off myself. He told me just how to take it off.

speaker: SAM
words: How had you busted it?

speaker: DB
words: Horse fell on me. Horse fell on this leg, and I had a steel stirrup.

speaker: DB
words: And here's what happened—it twisted my foot see, that steel stirrup.

speaker: DB
words: 'Course I had a horse a time or two to fall on me, but I don't know, that's

speaker: DB
words: the first time that, I don't know what happened. But anyhow, I thought the

speaker: DB
words: stirrup was straight, but it wasn't. It rolled, and when it rolled, well

speaker: DB
words: boy, it just twistey foot, an this heel right off right up through

speaker: DB
words: here to the joint. An broke this little joint out here, my little one,

speaker: DB
words: broke that one too, an busted this knuckle here. Boy I'll tell a man, it

speaker: DB
words: was mashed up. And there we had a very good doctor because, well I'll tell

speaker: DB
words: ya, he was a doctor there in Waymore. He was head physician there for the

speaker: DB
words: Burlington Railroad, he had a regular car there. They had a wreck, he had

speaker: DB
words: to go, see, and they'd take him right in the car. But of course we knowed

speaker: DB
words: him and we called him up, and he come right out and set it.

speaker: SAM
words: Did your folks move in pretty easily? Was it hard to make the change when

speaker: SAM
words: they got out here?

speaker: DB
words: No. We knowed so many people that was from the East right there where we

speaker: DB
words: was...Well, Dad and them was all raised together, lots of 'em, see. Well,

speaker: DB
words: fit right in, you know. Yah. Made it nice.

speaker: SAM
words: So when did you first start working in the woods?

speaker: DB
words: That fall. I got around to work a little that summer, and then I took in

speaker: DB
words: thrashing that fall. Well, one of the fellers that we knew in the Nebraska,

speaker: DB
words: he had a thrashing machine, so I went out with him, McFarland, an of course I knowed

speaker: DB
words: *em all there. And then that fall after thrashing was

speaker: DB
words: over we come in, it was timber there on the place. So Dad, he wasn't very,

speaker: DB
words: well he couldn't get around, he couldn't work much, so I hired a man to help

speaker: DB
words: me. And I sawed the logs, skidded fem, decked *em up, and got 'em ready for

speaker: DB
words: a sleigh haul that winter.

speaker: SAM
words: Had you ever worked in the woods before that?

speaker: DB
words: No, there was no woods there to work in.

speaker: SAM
words: That's what I figured.

speaker: DB
words: It was on the prairia, y'know. So I had to learn it all. And I started

speaker: DB
words: then to working in the woods. And well, the next year I went back out

speaker: DB
words: thrashing for him, and I come in and I logged that winter again down there

speaker: DB
words: on the place, sleigh hauled. And the next spring, why Hershiel, he went to

speaker: DB
words: Bovill, and I went up there, and I went to work up in the woods, and worked

speaker: DB
words: in the woods ever since.

speaker: SAM
words: Can you remember what it was like for you to learn, what the learning of

speaker: SAM
words: some of the things you had to figure out?

speaker: DB
words: Ohhh, yes. But, here's something about any job you go at. If you take an

speaker: DB
words: interest in it, you learn faster. Did ya know that? And if ya don't take

speaker: DB
words: a big interest in it, well hmmm, forget it, I don't care much about it, go

speaker: DB
words: on about something else. But it interested me, logging, and I kept right on.

speaker: DB
words: Well I done everything in the woods. There's only one thing that I never

speaker: DB
words: did do—drive a logging truck, and I didn't want to, never wanted to drive

speaker: DB
words: a logging truck. But I drove four horses on a sleigh and everything else,

speaker: DB
words: but a logging truck, no ma'am. But I unloaded logs here in Potlatch off

speaker: DB
words: of trucks for ten years. They'd run me down out of the woods to unload

speaker: DB
words: trucks see, and I unloaded them with a peavey then.

speaker: SAM
words: Can you remember—to go back to the first times that you were logging, can

speaker: SAM
words: you remember how you ran the operation then, what ya had to do?

speaker: DB
words: Oh sure, yeeaah (chuckles), ya just go at it and learn it, that's all there

speaker: DB
words: is to it. I got caught a time or two, I got broke up a time or two in the

speaker: DB
words: woods, but careful as you are, I don't care, you're gonna get caught sometime.

speaker: SAM
words: How many guys was doing it that first winter?

speaker: DB
words: Well, when we started logging down there, just dad and I. And I got another

speaker: DB
words: man to help us, and then him and I, we skidded and decked the logs. And that

speaker: DB
words: winter we sleigh hauled 'em, just the two of us, we just had one little team.

speaker: DB
words: We got us a nice little logging sled, and just got in what we could get in.

speaker: DB
words: We had all the money, wasn't out no money. Now Tribble down there, he went

speaker: DB
words: and put a big crew in, and he come up there after we got done logging, and

speaker: DB
words: he said "How come?"

speaker: DB
words: And I said, "How come? What ya mean?"

speaker: DB
words: He said, "You fellers made more money than I did this winter."

speaker: DB
words: And I said, "Well, maybe we did. I don't know," I said, "what we got,

speaker: DB
words: it's ours. We ain't out nothing for help to speak of." I forget, I believe

speaker: DB
words: I give him two and a half a day for helping me saw logs, to swamp 'em and

speaker: DB
words: buck 'em up. And then I skidded 'em all and decked 'em myself. Yah. That's

speaker: DB
words: the way I learnt (chuckles). Yeah. Well then, when I went to Bovill, I

speaker: DB
words: worked there with Eldon Tribble (Hershiel's brother) in the kitchen, and

speaker: DB
words: then I went out of that and want to scaling, and I scaled awhile and then I

speaker: DB
words: come down, worked down here almost a year in the woods. Then I went back up,

speaker: DB
words: because they shut this down again. This camp they had down below here a mile

speaker: DB
words: and a half—had a good logging camp outfit down there, just the same as the

speaker: DB
words: one over here in the flat. They shut that down and then I went back up and

speaker: DB
words: went to work in the woods. When I come back up that time I went into the

speaker: DB
words: donkey business, steam donkeys. Well I showed you a picture of one of them

speaker: DB
words: steam donkeys. It was all donkey work then up there. Yeah. Then they went

speaker: DB
words: out of that and went into horses again, and then they went out of that and

speaker: DB
words: went into caterpillar, and then they went back into horses again. y«ll, it

speaker: DB
words: was on to who was running the layout. And then finally they went into

speaker: DB
words: trucking, caterpillar and trucking, yah. Well it's just got bigger .and it's

speaker: DB
words: been that way ever since. But the horses and the railroad was the cheapest

speaker: DB
words: logging they ever done. I know, I was right here.

speaker: SAM
words: Why?

speaker: DB
words: Well, let's put it this way. If you've got a trucking road up here in the

speaker: DB
words: wintertime, how much is it gonna cost ya to keep that road up? Well if

speaker: DB
words: you got a railroad out there—of course, a railroad's expensive, I'll admit,

speaker: DB
words: but the way they done it, y'know, it wasn't too bad. Because they had a

speaker: DB
words: steam shovel, and they build a mile of grade

speaker: DB
words: a day, to lay the track on. Well, then they had Camp 12, it come along, and

speaker: DB
words: then laid a mile of steel a day, see. Well after they got that laid—we'll

speaker: DB
words: put it this way—after they got that laid, two or three men would keep that

speaker: DB
words: whole layout up. Well, you take a truckroad, look at the rigging, Isok what

speaker: DB
words: it cost you to keep that road up! Yeah, that's it. Yeah, that's the whole

speaker: DB
words: setup right there. And here at one time, y'know, they had well about 42 mile

speaker: DB
words: of steel laid in here, railroad track. They was logging on it, and they used

speaker: DB
words: 20 teams all the time, and of course we put in a lot of logs.

speaker: SAM
words: When did they start doing that in here?

speaker: DB
words: Ohhh, let's see, I believe it was in '20, about in 1919 I think, or some

speaker: DB
words: where along there, they put the first camp in here and started logging.

speaker: SAM
words: How long did the thing go on?

speaker: DB
words: Well, they moved out in '27, '23, but he kept 20 teams in here and kept

speaker: DB
words: 'em busy all the time.

speaker: SAM
words: This is the same camp right across the way here?

speaker: DB
words: Well, no. Now before, I'll tell ya what happened. I was working up there

speaker: DB
words: at Old Camp 2 above Bovill, when they's talking logging and he was coming

speaker: DB
words: down, and they come to find out they couldn't get Camp 2 through the rail

speaker: DB
words: roa

speaker: DB
words: ridge at Harvard. She was too wide, she wouldn't come through the

speaker: DB
words: railroa

speaker: DB
words: ridge So they turned around, they wanted these logs out of

speaker: DB
words: here, so they turned around an

speaker: DB
words: uilt a ground camp down here about a mile

speaker: DB
words: and a quarter—they built just a whole ground camp. Well, then, while they

speaker: DB
words: were doing that and we's logging there, then they built that camp that I

speaker: DB
words: just showed you here, at Potlatch. Well, they built it so it'd go through

speaker: DB
words: the railroad ridge. Well then when they got done here, they took the same

speaker: DB
words: camp that we used here, they took it over there on Flat Creek, because they

speaker: DB
words: could get it through the bridge.

speaker: SAM
words: Um-hm. So it was all to get through the bridge.

speaker: DB
words: They built one they could go through the bridge with see. So they never

speaker: DB
words: thought about it 'til, well, they were all ready to move and everything. And

speaker: DB
words: Dones, he'd come down here and go to logging. An Nogle, he

speaker: DB
words: come along and he said, "We can't do that." "Hmm. Why?"

speaker: DB
words: He said, "If we're gonna do that we're gonna have to tear that bridge

speaker: DB
words: down or how else," he said, "would you get it through there? She won't

speaker: DB
words: go through."

speaker: DB
words: "How do you know?" he said.

speaker: DB
words: "I was down and measured it." (Chuckles.) Well, it was a good thing

speaker: DB
words: they didn't get at that before he'd come and measured it. Yah.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, when they was logging in here, did they take everything, or did they

speaker: SAM
words: just take the big stuff, or how was it done?

speaker: DB
words: Oh, they took everything, everything. I'll tell ya one thing that did

speaker: DB
words: happen while they was at it—why, Dapan bought a lot of timber off of 'em.

speaker: DB
words: Dack Donovan was check scaler, and he come down one day, and of course he

speaker: DB
words: was check scaling while I was scaling up there. And he said, "Dick," he

speaker: DB
words: said, "don't take that in."

speaker: DB
words: And I said, "What's the matter with it?"

speaker: DB
words: And he said, "That's a cull log."

speaker: DB
words: "How do you figure that, Dack?"

speaker: DB
words: "Well," he said, sit down on this log now I'll explain it." He said,

speaker: DB
words: "You know, we're selling a lot of timber to Dapan." And he said, "They're

speaker: DB
words: getting it over on the coast." And he said, "They're putting it aboard the

speaker: DB
words: boat cheaper than we can saw it and ship it there." Well what it was, it

speaker: DB
words: was all big timbers, just squared up, and they take it to Dapan, y'know,

speaker: DB
words: and they whipsaw it. One gets above and one below and they whip her off by

speaker: DB
words: hand. And they just took her over there and sunk her, and if they wanted it,

speaker: DB
words: why they'd raise her up and saw her up by hand. So, they left some good

speaker: DB
words: timber up here that made good wood (chuckles). Yeah.

speaker: DB
words: Well, you know, it's this way, y'know, I was down there with the son,

speaker: DB
words: he works for Simpson, in that redwood. 'Course he's been down there now for

speaker: DB
words: Simpson I guess about, oh about 20 years I guess, 19, 20 years worked right

speaker: DB
words: there at that one place for old Simpson. And we was down there this spring,

speaker: DB
words: and I was talking to him, and I was talking to the boss. They on sending Dapan logs.

speaker: DB
words: And this feller said to me, he said, "I don't see

speaker: DB
words: how we can stop it."

speaker: DB
words: And I said, "How do you mean?"

speaker: DB
words: "Well," he said, they just come over here with $21,800,000, bought

speaker: DB
words: land, timber and all." He said, "How we gonna keep from handling their own

speaker: DB
words: timber?" (Chuckles.) Well that's the way this big business is going.

speaker: DB
words: Yeah.

speaker: SAM
words: Well how long ago was this? This was in the '20's that they were shipping

speaker: SAM
words: to Dapan too right?

speaker: DB
words: Why sure! They have been.

speaker: SAM
words: Nothing new.

speaker: DB
words: Nothing new. Why no, we were shipping to Dapan during World War I!

speaker: DB
words: Yeah, they was shipping over there then. Shipping all of the lumber they

speaker: DB
words: wanted. 'Course at that time they didn't ship 'em logs, they shipped 'em

speaker: DB
words: cants. They squared 'em up and canted them out and shipped 'em over there.

speaker: DB
words: So, how ya gonna stop it? We can't stop it. It's just like this way, you

speaker: DB
words: know. My wife was cooking over there at the University at Moscow. And

speaker: DB
words: there was a lad I got pretty well acquainted with, and he come over here,

speaker: DB
words: him and his lady friend (that was before he was married), they'd come over

speaker: DB
words: here, and they was over here several different times. Well we got a letter

speaker: DB
words: from him this Christmas, and he's 35 miles south of the equator. Well, it's

speaker: DB
words: the way that big monied outfit operates. And he told us, he said, "I'm

speaker: DB
words: a-drivin' a helicopter, logging with it. A big one." And he said,"Weyerhaeusers,"

speaker: DB
words: he said "tell Dick," he said, "Weyerhaeusers owns 85/6 of it." And he said,

speaker: DB
words: "The army here on this island owns the other 15$i". Yeah. Well, now this

speaker: DB
words: feller that the boy works for there in California, that Simpson, Weyerhaeuser

speaker: DB
words: and him's related, and they work together. They got two big mills there,

speaker: DB
words: bigger than that'n in Lewiston, and just a highway run between 'em. And

speaker: DB
words: they'll take one load of logs to one, they'll take it there; we'll take one

speaker: DB
words: load of logs to the other, and they take it there. So what ya gonna do?

speaker: DB
words: But when he went in the islands, Weyerhaeuser went to the islands, what

speaker: DB
words: did Simpson do—he moved three mills into Alaska. You get the idea? They

speaker: DB
words: got it on both ends, haven't they? (Laughs.) And that's just the way it

speaker: DB
words: works. Yeah. Yes sir, that's just the way it works.

speaker: DB
words: Well, you know when I was scaling up there (between Santa and Fernwood),

speaker: DB
words: I worked there at the mill. Well there's the picture of it, I'll show it to

speaker: DB
words: ya. Here. I worked here at this mill 19 years. Well in fact therer was

speaker: DB
words: three sawmills there. I'll show you how it is. You see here is the Milwaukee

speaker: DB
words: Railroad right down through here. Here's a sawmill, here's a sawmill, here's

speaker: DB
words: a sawmill and here's a sawmill here, and here is the planer. And this is

speaker: DB
words: sawlogs here, and that's lumber. So. Now this Fairhurst from Portland, he

speaker: DB
words: started all that operation, furnished the money for it. But now it's all

speaker: DB
words: tore up. But we've got the mill (let me get it better on my glasses), but

speaker: DB
words: they don't saw any more lumber, they saw chips. That Scott Paper Company

speaker: DB
words: got it. And the mill sets here, and they just cut the chips an in here. Now, here's

speaker: DB
words: something else, y'know. Weyerhaeuser was gonna put

speaker: DB
words: in a chipper at Clarkia, because this outfit here didn't handle big enough

speaker: DB
words: logs. So they said "All right. We'll put in a chipper in Clarkia that'll

speaker: DB
words: handle it." (I'll get that back up there. He hangs the picture up.) And

speaker: DB
words: he said, "If you fellers don't want it," he said, "we're gonna take our chips

speaker: DB
